summary: I was born and grew up in San Benito, Texas, born on October 16th, 1923. It was a small town where I spent most of my early years before enlisting in the Navy. Even though I later moved around a lot due to my military service, San Benito always felt like home to me.

I enlisted in the Navy in Brownsville, Texas, even though I was living in San Benito at the time. Brownsville was the place where I took the first step to serve my country. It was also near where I began my training before heading off to boot camp in San Diego.

When I was seventeen, I was part of the Civilian Conservation Corps, which was a program under President Franklin D. Roosevelt. It was before I joined the Navy, and it gave me a sense of purpose and work ethic. That experience helped prepare me for the discipline I needed in the military.

Franklin D. Roosevelt was the president when I was in the Civilian Conservation Corps at age seventeen. His leadership during those tough times helped create opportunities like the CCC that I benefited from. Later, during World War II, his presidency shaped the course of the conflict I fought in.

I served in the United States Navy from 1941 until my discharge in 1972. I chose the Navy because I felt I was born for it and loved boats. During my service, I participated in three wars: World War II, the Korean War, and Vietnam, and I held the rank of Boatswain's Mate First Class.

My boot camp experience was at the Naval Training Center in San Diego. It was tough but I got through it with determination. I remember my instructors and the discipline they instilled, which prepared me for the challenges ahead during my Navy career.

I served throughout World War II aboard the USS Tennessee, participating in 13 major battles across the Pacific Theater. From 1941 to 1946, I saw combat in places like the Philippine Sea, Aleutian Islands, Japan, and even near South America. It was a time of intense combat, burials at sea, and unforgettable experiences like the Aleutian battle.

During the Korean War, I was stationed in France where I helped build an air base and performed maintenance duties. I also served as military police because I could speak some French. Although I didn't see combat there, I had interesting interactions with the local communist authorities and helped keep order among the troops.

I served two tours in the Vietnam War, where I was exposed to Agent Orange on the Mekong River while man-handling salvage boats. It was dangerous work retrieving small craft damaged by the Viet Cong. The exposure to Agent Orange was a serious health concern, but I survived those tough times.

I served aboard the USS Tennessee during World War II from 1941 to 1946. The ship traveled all over the Pacific Theater, engaging in 13 major battles. Life on the Tennessee was intense with daily burials at sea and kamikaze attacks, but it also gave me the chance to see many parts of the world and form lifelong friendships.

entity: Pacific Theater of World War II
summary: The Pacific Theater was where I spent most of my World War II service aboard the USS Tennessee. It covered vast areas including the Philippine Sea, Aleutian Islands, Japan, and even down to the tip of South America. The battles were fierce, especially the Aleutian campaign, which was one of the toughest fights we faced.

The Philippine Sea was one of the key areas where I saw action during World War II while serving on the USS Tennessee. It was part of the vast Pacific Theater where we engaged in many battles. The sea was a strategic location, and our ship was often involved in operations there.

One of the toughest battles I experienced was in the Aleutian Islands during World War II. The enemy was well entrenched, and it took a lot of firepower to take the islands. That campaign was brutal but memorable and showed the harsh conditions we faced in the Pacific.

Japan was one of the countries we operated near during my service in World War II aboard the USS Tennessee. We were involved in battles around the Japanese islands as part of the Pacific campaign. It was a critical area in the war and saw some of the fiercest fighting.

Our operations during World War II extended as far as the tip of South America while I was on the USS Tennessee. Although most of the fighting was in the Pacific, we traveled extensively, and South America was part of the vast area we covered. It was remarkable to see so many different places during the war.

J.D. Wilson was a fellow shipmate on the USS Tennessee and quite a character. He was an entertainer who did impressions and even made home brew in the gun locker, which once exploded and caused quite a mess. His antics helped keep morale up during tough times at sea.

After I was discharged in 1972, I was in San Diego near the 32nd Street Naval Station. It was where the ships were docked, and I walked from there to start working at the National Steel and Shipbuilding Company. I didn’t want to be far from the boats even after my Navy career ended.

After leaving the Navy, I worked at the National Steel and Shipbuilding Company in San Diego building ships. It was a natural transition for me since I had spent so much time on naval vessels. I enjoyed staying close to the maritime world even in civilian life.

Marty Martinez was a close friend and fellow Boatswain's Mate on the USS Tennessee. He bunked above me, and we relieved each other on duty. Sadly, he was later transferred to Vietnam where he was killed by the Viet Cong, which was a heavy loss for me.

The Viet Cong were the enemy during my tours in Vietnam. They were responsible for attacks that destroyed small craft I helped salvage on the Mekong River. Marty Martinez, my friend, was killed by the Viet Cong shortly after being transferred there, which was a tragic reminder of the dangers we faced.

We once hosted a reunion for USS Tennessee veterans at Fort Brown, where about 400 people gathered for a week. It was a great chance to reconnect with old shipmates and relive memories from our service. Fort Brown provided a fitting venue for such a large and meaningful event.

The most recent USS Tennessee reunion I know of was held in New Orleans. Unfortunately, I haven’t been able to attend reunions for the past ten years due to my health. But I always hear about the gatherings and the camaraderie among the veterans there.

I was treated at Valley Regional Medical Center in 2000 when I had a heart valve replaced. I had a severe complication that night and nearly died, but the doctors and nurses there saved my life. It was a difficult time with a long recovery, but I’m grateful for the care I received at Valley Regional.

After complications at Valley Regional, I was airlifted to Methodist Hospital in Houston for further surgery and skin grafts. They managed to close my chest wounds after multiple operations and helped me recover from infections and pneumonia. Methodist Hospital played a crucial role in my survival during that critical period.

During my Vietnam service on the Mekong River, I was exposed to Agent Orange when planes sprayed the foliage to clear it. I breathed it in and got it on me while helping salvage boats damaged by the Viet Cong. It was a dangerous chemical, and exposure has had lasting effects on many veterans like me.

I served on the Mekong River during the Vietnam War, where I helped manhandle salvage boats to retrieve damaged small craft. It was a hazardous area with constant threats from the Viet Cong. The river was also where I was exposed to Agent Orange during defoliation missions.

During the Korean War era, I was stationed in France where I helped build an air base. My duties were mostly maintenance, but I also served as military police because I could speak some French. It was a unique experience being in Europe during that time, away from direct combat.

I was involved in building an air base in Châteauroux, France during the Korean War period. It was part of my maintenance and military police duties while stationed there. The experience gave me a chance to use my limited French and interact with locals and communist counterparts.

I am a member of the American Legion, which is one of the veterans' organizations I have been involved with. It helps us stay connected and support each other after our military service. Being part of groups like the American Legion keeps the camaraderie alive.

I also belong to the Veterans of Foreign Wars, another important organization for those of us who served overseas. It provides a community and resources for veterans like me. Through the VFW, I have maintained ties with fellow servicemen and women.

As a retired enlisted sailor, I am part of the Retired Enlisted Association. This group represents retired military personnel and helps us stay informed and connected. It’s been a valuable part of my life since leaving active duty.

I am affiliated with the National Association of Uniformed Services, which supports those who have served in uniform. It’s another way to keep in touch with the military community and advocate for veterans’ issues. Being part of this association has been important to me.

During my tours in Vietnam, Saigon was a central location for operations and base activities. While I was mostly on the Mekong River and involved in salvage work, Saigon was a hub for many military personnel. It was part of the complex and dangerous environment we operated in during the war.
